worth_it_explanation: IAS Signal is the reporting platform where everything the IAS deployment measures comes together: viewability, invalid traffic (IVT), brand safety, and suitability. When Signal is not structured to match the media plan, its reporting cannot be read at the level a practitioner needs to act on it. When measurement tags or DSP integrations are missing, Signal is counting only a fraction of the actual spend, and the fractions it misses are invisible rather than flagged. Fixing the setup is a one-time configuration effort that determines whether the rest of the IAS audit findings are trustworthy.
intro:
Integral Ad Science (IAS) Signal is the unified reporting interface for IAS measurement data: viewability, invalid traffic filtration, brand safety and suitability, and pre-bid avoidance segment performance. IAS measurement works by attaching a JavaScript tag to the ad creative or by receiving server-to-server impression data from a demand-side platform (DSP). Both paths send data to Signal, where it is organized by the campaign structure the practitioner builds inside the platform.

A Signal deployment can fail at three points before a practitioner ever opens a report. The first is structural: if Signal is not organized to mirror the media plan, its filters and breakdowns do not line up with the campaigns and channels the practitioner is responsible for, and the data becomes difficult to read and act on. The second is coverage: if some active campaigns do not have IAS tags or an active DSP integration, those impressions do not appear in Signal at all, and the unmeasured share creates a blind spot rather than a clearly labeled gap. The third is the data connection itself: DSP integrations that are misconfigured or inactive stop delivering impression data to Signal, leaving the reporting stale or incomplete without any error visible to the practitioner.

These three checks confirm that the IAS Signal setup matches the shape of the media plan, that measurement covers the active campaigns, and that the data feeds supplying Signal are active and current.

audit_checks.title: IAS Signal is not structured to mirror the media plan, so reporting cannot be segmented by campaign or channel
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When Signal's campaign structure does not match the active media plan, viewability, IVT, and brand-safety data cannot be read at the campaign or channel level. A practitioner reviewing Signal sees aggregate numbers that mix unrelated campaigns together, making it difficult to identify which buys are underperforming and which are clean. The data is present but not actionable.

educate.base:
IAS Signal organizes measurement data into a hierarchy that mirrors the campaign structure of the media buy. At the top level, Signal holds advertisers or accounts. Below that, it holds campaigns, and below campaigns it holds individual placements or line items. The reporting filters and breakdowns in Signal operate against this hierarchy, so a practitioner can only slice the data as finely as the structure allows.

When the Signal structure is built without reference to the actual media plan, mismatches appear in two forms. The first is consolidation: multiple distinct campaigns or channels are grouped under a single Signal campaign, so their IVT or viewability data cannot be separated even when the practitioner needs to compare them. The second is fragmentation: a single campaign is split across multiple Signal campaigns for no reason tied to the media plan, which means the total figures for that campaign require manual addition across rows.

Both problems produce the same outcome: the Signal report does not answer the questions the practitioner brings to it. A media buy that runs across display, video, and connected television (CTV) needs Signal to show each channel separately so the viewability rates, which differ significantly by format, can be interpreted correctly.

A few signs suggest the Signal structure does not match the media plan:
  • Signal campaign names do not correspond to the names used in the DSP or the media plan document
  • A Signal campaign that spans multiple channels (display, video, and CTV mixed together) shows a blended viewability rate with no way to separate the formats
  • Active campaigns visible in the DSP are not represented by any Signal campaign, suggesting they were omitted when the structure was built
You may want to open Signal and compare the campaign list to the active line items in the DSP or the current media plan before evaluating the measurement data underneath them.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running display prospecting, video retargeting, and a separate CTV brand campaign, each channel should map to its own Signal campaign so viewability rates, which differ by format and environment, can be read separately. You may want to confirm that Signal campaigns do not bundle the prospecting and retargeting buys together. Mixing audiences and formats in one Signal campaign makes the performance data harder to act on.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying display and video across multiple publishers, the Signal structure should reflect how the media plan separates the investment. You may want to confirm that campaigns with different performance goals, such as awareness video versus direct-response display, are held in separate Signal campaigns so their IVT and viewability results can be evaluated against the correct benchmarks.

educate.learn_more_note: Step-by-step guidance on building and organizing campaigns inside IAS Signal is available in the IAS advertiser help center. Access requires an active IAS account login through the IAS Signal platform (helpcenter.integralplatform.com). Your IAS account team can also walk through Signal structure recommendations as part of onboarding or account review.

investigate.intro: Compare the campaign structure in IAS Signal to the active line items in the DSP and the current media plan.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and open the campaign list for the advertiser account. Note the names of all active campaigns.
Open your DSP or media plan and list the active campaigns or line items currently running. Compare this list to the Signal campaign names.

For any Signal campaign that combines more than one channel, such as display and video in a single campaign, note whether the reporting view allows you to filter or break down by format. If it does not, the structure cannot separate the format-specific data.
For any active DSP campaign with no matching Signal campaign, note it as a potential coverage gap to confirm in the next check.
Confirm whether Signal campaign names match the naming convention used in the DSP or media plan. Consistent naming is the primary way to confirm the structures align.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether each active DSP campaign or media plan line item corresponds to a distinct Signal campaign, and whether Signal campaign names match those in the DSP or media plan. A Signal structure that maps one-to-one with the active media plan and uses consistent naming passes. A structure that consolidates multiple distinct campaigns into one Signal campaign, or that uses names with no recognizable connection to the active media plan, is a fail, recorded with the specific mismatches.
evaluate.fallback_message: List the Signal campaigns and the corresponding DSP campaigns or media plan line items above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the IAS Signal campaign structure for {{site_name}} does not mirror the active media plan. Signal campaigns either consolidate multiple distinct channels or campaigns into a single entry, or use names that do not correspond to the campaigns running in the DSP. As a result, the viewability, invalid traffic (IVT), and brand-safety data in Signal cannot be read at the campaign or channel level needed to act on it. {{voice_recommend}} rebuilding the Signal campaign structure to match the active media plan, with one Signal campaign per distinct DSP campaign or channel grouping, and aligning Signal campaign names to the naming convention used in the buying platform.

audit_checks.title: IAS measurement is not deployed on all active campaigns, leaving a share of impressions unmeasured
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When IAS tags or DSP integrations are absent from some campaigns, the impressions those campaigns serve do not appear in Signal at all. A practitioner reviewing Signal cannot see that coverage is partial; the unmeasured campaigns simply do not show up. IVT, viewability, and brand-safety data drawn from the measured share may not reflect the full buy, and the unmeasured campaigns carry no protection against invalid traffic during the flight.

educate.base:
IAS measures an ad impression through one of two paths. The first is a JavaScript tag attached to the ad creative. When the creative loads in a browser or an application that supports the IAB Open Measurement Software Development Kit (OM SDK), the tag fires and sends impression-level data to IAS Signal: the environment, the viewability outcome, and the IVT classification. The second path is a server-to-server integration between a DSP and IAS. In this model, the DSP sends impression data directly to IAS, and IAS applies its measurement logic to the data feed. Both paths require deliberate setup and ongoing maintenance.

Coverage gaps appear when active campaigns do not have either path configured. A campaign bought through a DSP that has a working server-to-server integration with IAS but was not linked to the IAS account will not send data. A campaign using a creative that does not carry the IAS tag will not be measured through the tag path. A campaign running in a mobile in-app environment on apps that have not integrated the OM SDK will not return viewability data even if the tag is present. The tag cannot fire inside a native app environment without the SDK integrated by the app publisher.

The Media Rating Council (MRC) accredits IAS for viewability and sophisticated invalid traffic (SIVT) detection across display, video, connected television (CTV), and mobile environments, with the caveat that CTV accreditation applies to certified traffic only. Accreditation confirms that IAS's measurement methodology meets the MRC's standards, but accreditation does not mean IAS can measure an impression if the tag or integration was never deployed.

A few signs suggest coverage is not complete:
  • A campaign visible in the DSP shows no corresponding impressions in Signal during its flight dates
  • The impression volume in Signal for a given campaign is substantially lower than the impression count in the DSP for the same period
  • Signal shows a high rate of unmeasured impressions for a mobile campaign, which may reflect in-app environments where the OM SDK is not present
You may want to confirm, for each active campaign, which measurement path is active and whether Signal is receiving impression data at the expected volume.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser running campaigns across display, video, and mobile, coverage gaps appear most often in mobile in-app placements where apps have not integrated the OM SDK. You may want to confirm that the IAS mobile in-app measurement path is active for any campaign buying in-app inventory, and check Signal for an unmeasured impression rate that is higher in mobile than in other environments.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser buying across multiple DSPs, coverage gaps appear when only one DSP is integrated with IAS and others are not. You may want to confirm that every DSP buying media on behalf of the advertiser has an active connection to the IAS account, and that Signal is receiving impression data from each platform.

learn_more.source_title: Digital measurement accreditation: MRC accredited services, including IAS viewability and IVT
Open Measurement SDK compliance: enabling in-app viewability measurement

educate.learn_more_note: Guidance on tag deployment, DSP integration setup, and confirming coverage within IAS Signal is available in the IAS advertiser help center, which requires an active IAS account login (helpcenter.integralplatform.com). Your IAS account team can run a coverage audit to identify unmeasured campaigns.

investigate.intro: Compare impression volumes in IAS Signal to those in the DSP for the same campaigns and flight dates to identify where coverage is absent or substantially lower than expected.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Open IAS Signal and navigate to the reporting view for the advertiser. Filter to the current or most recent campaign flight dates.
For each active campaign, note the total impressions IAS measured. Then open the same campaign in the DSP and note the total impressions served for the same date range.

Calculate the difference between DSP-served impressions and IAS-measured impressions. A difference greater than roughly 5 to 10 percent warrants investigation. Some discrepancy is expected due to measurement timing and filtering, so larger gaps point to a coverage problem rather than normal variance.
If Signal shows zero impressions for a campaign that the DSP confirms is active, that campaign has no IAS measurement path configured.

For mobile campaigns, check Signal for the unmeasured impression rate. A high unmeasured rate in mobile often reflects in-app environments where the OM SDK is not present on the publisher side, so the IAS tag cannot fire.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each active campaign, the IAS-measured impression count and the DSP-served impression count for the same period. A campaign where Signal impressions are within roughly 10 percent of DSP impressions, with no unexplained zero-measurement campaigns, passes. A campaign where Signal shows zero impressions, or where the gap between Signal and DSP impressions exceeds roughly 10 percent without a documented reason, is a fail, recorded with the campaign name and the magnitude of the discrepancy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS Signal impression count and DSP impression count for each active campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that IAS measurement is not deployed on all active campaigns in {{site_name}}'s account. One or more campaigns show either zero IAS-measured impressions or a substantial gap between the impression count in the DSP and the count in IAS Signal, indicating that those campaigns are running without an active IAS tag or DSP integration. The viewability, invalid traffic (IVT), and brand-safety data in Signal does not reflect the full media buy. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the measurement path, tag or server-to-server integration, for each active campaign, deploying IAS measurement on any campaign that currently lacks it, and reviewing Signal's unmeasured impression rate for mobile campaigns to determine whether in-app coverage requires the OM SDK on the publisher side.

audit_checks.title: Data connections from buying platforms to IAS Signal are missing or misconfigured, making Signal reporting incomplete
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a DSP's data connection to IAS Signal is absent or inactive, Signal does not receive impression data from that platform. Campaigns bought through that DSP appear to have no IAS data, or Signal's totals for those campaigns stop updating. The practitioner cannot distinguish a silent data-feed failure from a campaign that has genuinely stopped delivering, which delays identifying real problems.

educate.base:
IAS Signal receives programmatic impression data through two mechanisms. The first is creative-level tagging: the IAS JavaScript tag fires when a tagged creative renders, and the tag sends data to Signal independently of the DSP. The second is a server-to-server data connection between the DSP and IAS, where the DSP transmits impression logs directly to IAS. The two paths can be used together, with tagging covering direct buys and data connections covering programmatic buys made through a DSP.

A server-to-server data connection requires a one-time setup step: the advertiser links their DSP account to their IAS account, typically by entering credentials or generating an authorization token within Signal or the DSP's integrations panel. Once active, the connection runs automatically. When the connection is not set up, or when it lapses due to a credential expiration or a platform change, Signal stops receiving data from that DSP without displaying an error visible to the practitioner.

A lapsed or missing data connection produces reporting that goes stale: Signal's impression counts for campaigns bought through that DSP stop updating at the point the connection broke, while the DSP continues to serve and record impressions. The practitioner sees Signal data that no longer reflects the current flight, and the gap between Signal and the DSP widens each day the connection is inactive.

A few signs point to a data-connection problem:
  • Signal shows impressions for a DSP-bought campaign through a past date but no impressions after a specific date, while the DSP confirms the campaign continued serving
  • A DSP is listed in the IAS integrations panel but shows as inactive or disconnected
  • Signal shows impressions for some DSPs but not for others that are actively buying media for the same advertiser
You may want to review the integrations or data-connection settings in IAS Signal to confirm which DSPs are actively sending data and when each connection last received a successful data update.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser buying across multiple DSPs, each platform needs its own active data connection in Signal. You may want to confirm that Signal shows current impression data for each DSP the account uses, and check whether any DSP shows impressions through a past date with no data after, which would indicate a lapsed connection rather than a campaign that stopped running.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser centralizing programmatic buying through a single DSP, a lapsed data connection leaves the entire programmatic buy invisible in Signal. You may want to confirm that the DSP integration is active and that Signal's impression data for programmatic campaigns is current within the expected reporting lag.

educate.learn_more_note: Configuration steps for connecting a DSP account to IAS Signal, including the credentials and authorization steps required for each supported platform, are available in the IAS Signal help center. Access requires an active IAS account login (helpcenter.integralplatform.com). The IAS Signal platform also shows the status and last-updated timestamp for each active data connection in its integrations or settings panel.

investigate.intro: Review the data connections in IAS Signal to confirm which DSPs are actively sending data and whether the connection for each has updated recently.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the account settings, integrations, or data connections panel. Note which DSPs are listed and the status shown for each connection.
For any DSP showing an inactive or error status, note the date of the last successful data update and the platform involved.

In Signal's reporting view, open a campaign that is currently active in a DSP and check the date of the most recent impression data. If the last impression date is more than one to two days before today, the connection may be stale.

Compare the list of DSPs with active Signal connections to the list of DSPs currently buying media for the advertiser. Any DSP buying media that is not represented in Signal's connections list is a gap.

If a DSP connection shows as active in Signal but the impression volume in Signal is substantially lower than the DSP reports for the same campaign and date range, the connection may be partially broken or throttled.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each DSP buying media for the advertiser, whether an active data connection exists in IAS Signal and whether Signal shows impression data current within the expected reporting lag. A DSP with an active connection and impression data updated within one to two days of today passes. A DSP buying media for the advertiser with no Signal connection, an inactive status, or impression data that stops before the current flight date is a fail, recorded with the DSP name and the date the data stops updating.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the connection status and last-update date for each DSP in IAS Signal above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more of the data connections between {{site_name}}'s buying platforms and IAS Signal are missing or no longer active. Signal's impression data for campaigns bought through the affected DSP stops at a past date, while the DSP confirms those campaigns continued to serve. The IAS viewability, invalid traffic (IVT), and brand-safety data in Signal does not reflect the current state of the buy, and the gap between Signal and the DSP widens each day the connection remains inactive. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the integrations or data-connection panel in IAS Signal, reconnecting any DSP that shows an inactive or error status, and confirming that Signal's impression data for each connected platform is current within the expected reporting lag before relying on Signal data to evaluate campaign quality.
